24.89% ([[Nielsen ratings]]){{cite news|url=https://variety.com/2010/tv/markets-festivals/fox-tops-abc-s-big-week-1118016263/|title=FOX tops ABC's big week|last=Kissell|first=Rick|date=March 9, 2010|access-date=March 9, 2010|work=Variety|publisher=Penske Media Corporation|archive-date=November 16, 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181116094550/https://variety.com/2010/tv/markets-festivals/fox-tops-abc-s-big-week-1118016263/|url-status=live}} | last = 81st |next = 83rd }} The '''82nd Academy Awards''' ceremony, presented by the [[Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences]] (AMPAS), honored the best [[2009 in film|films of 2009]] and took place on March 7, 2010, at the [[Kodak Theatre]] in [[Hollywood, Los Angeles]], beginning at 5:30 p.m. [[Pacific Time Zone|PST]] / 8:30 p.m. [[Eastern Time Zone|EST]]. The ceremony was scheduled after its usual late-February date to avoid conflicting with the [[2010 Winter Olympics]].{{cite news|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/news/worldnews/northamerica/usa/5052370/Oscars-ceremony-moved-to-prevent-clash-with-Winter-Olympics.html |title=Oscars ceremony moved to prevent clash with Winter Olympics |date=March 26, 2009 |last=Hedley |first=Caroline |work=[[The Daily Telegraph]] |access-date=March 13, 2010 |location=London |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121113185305/http://www.telegraph.co.uk/news/worldnews/northamerica/usa/5052370/Oscars-ceremony-moved-to-prevent-clash-with-Winter-Olympics.html |archive-date=November 13, 2012 }} During the ceremony, the Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences presented [[Academy Awards]] (commonly referred to as Oscars) in 24 categories. The ceremony was televised in the United States by [[American Broadcasting Company|ABC]], and was produced by [[Bill Mechanic]] and [[Adam Shankman]] and directed by [[Hamish Hamilton (director)|Hamish Hamilton]]. Actors [[Alec Baldwin]] and [[Steve Martin]] hosted the show. Martin hosted for the third time; he first presided over the [[73rd Academy Awards|73rd ceremony]] held in 2001 and last hosted the [[75th Academy Awards|75th ceremony]] held in 2003, while this was Baldwin's first Oscars hosting stint. This was also the first telecast to have multiple hosts since the [[59th Academy Awards|59th ceremony]] held in 1987.{{cite news|title=Oscars try the buddy system for host this year |access-date=February 7, 2010 |date=February 1, 2010 |publisher=[[CTV News]] ([[Bell Media]]) |first=Constance |last=Droganes |url=http://www.ctvnews.ca/oscars-try-the-buddy-system-for-host-this-year-1.478885 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140408221643/http://www.ctvnews.ca/oscars-try-the-buddy-system-for-host-this-year-1.478885 |archive-date=April 8, 2014 }} On June 24, 2009, Academy president [[Sid Ganis]] announced at a press conference that, in an attempt to revitalize interest surrounding the awards, the 2010 ceremony would feature ten [[Academy Award for Best Picture|Best Picture]] nominees instead of five,{{cite news|url=http://www.oscars.org/press/pressreleases/2009/20090624.html |title=82nd Academy Awards to Feature 10 Best Picture Nominees |publisher=[[Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences]] (AMPAS) |date=June 24, 2009 |access-date=April 9, 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100408200952/http://www.oscars.org/press/pressreleases/2009/20090624.html |archive-date=April 8, 2010 |url-status=live }} a practice that had been discontinued after the [[16th Academy Awards|16th ceremony]] in 1944. He wears a suit and glasses. | caption2 = | footer = [[Alec Baldwin]] (''left'') and [[Steve Martin]] (''right'') co-hosted the 82nd Academy Awards. }} Because of the declining viewership of recent Academy Awards ceremonies, the Academy sought ideas to revamp the show while renewing interest with the nominated films. After the [[81st Academy Awards|previous year's telecast]], which saw a 13% increase in viewership, many within the Motion Picture Academy proposed new ways to give the awards a more populist appeal. AMPAS then-president [[Sid Ganis]] announced that the ceremony would feature ten Best Picture nominees, rather than traditional five. The expansion was a throwback to the Academy's early years in the [[1930s in film|1930s]] and [[1940s in film|1940s]], when eight to twelve films were nominated.{{cite web|last=Cieply |first=Michael |title=Academy Expands Best-Picture Pool to 10 |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2009/06/25/movies/25oscars.html |work=The New York Times |access-date=June 5, 2013 |date=June 24, 2009 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130717165526/http://www.nytimes.com/2009/06/25/movies/25oscars.html |archive-date=July 17, 2013 }} "Having 10 Best Picture nominees is going to allow Academy voters to recognize and include some of the fantastic movies that often show up in the other Oscar categories, but have been squeezed out of the race for the top prize," Sid Ganis said in a press conference. "I can't wait to see what that list of ten looks like when the nominees are announced in February." Ganis also said that became difficult to get a clear winner. A cause of this was required a change in the voting system from [[first-past-the-post]] to [[alternative vote]].{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/entertainment/8539436.stm |title=BBC News: Oscars 2010: Best picture voting changes explained |last=Smith |first=Neil |publisher=[[BBC]] |work=[[BBC News]] |date=March 1, 2010 |access-date=March 9, 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100311070948/http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/entertainment/8539436.stm |archive-date=March 11, 2010 |url-status=live }} [[Choreographer]] [[Adam Shankman]] and [[Bill Mechanic]] were hired as producers for the ceremony. Shankman revealed in an interview on [[NPR]]'s ''[[Fresh Air]]'' that he and Mechanic had originally chosen [[Sacha Baron Cohen]] as the host, but the Academy rejected this proposal because Baron Cohen was "too much of a wild card."{{cite web|url=https://www.npr.org/templates/transcript/transcript.php?storyId=123844321 |title=Shankman's Winning Job: Producing The Oscars |date=February 18, 2010 |publisher=[[NPR]] (National Public Radio, Inc.) |access-date=February 20, 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100304060450/http://www.npr.org/templates/transcript/transcript.php?storyId=123844321 |archive-date=March 4, 2010 |url-status=live }} Many of the previous year's well-received elements returned. Five actors with a personal connection with each of the nominees presented the Best Actor and Best Actress awards. Shankman and Mechanic announced their intention to make the running time of the telecast shorter.{{cite magazine|title=Looking Forward to The Big Show|first=Nicole |last=Sperling|magazine=[[Entertainment Weekly]]|publisher=[[Time Inc.]]|date=February 12, 2010}} Most presenters this year introduced each winner with the phrase "And the winner is ..." rather than "And the Oscar goes to..." for the first time since 1988. The Academy gave no reason for the change to a phrase which it had once felt humiliating to the other nominees; but apparently acquiesced in Shankman and Mechanic's decision to return to the older phrase.{{cite magazine|url=http://www.time.com/time/arts/article/0,8599,1970672,00.html |title=An Oscar Comeback: 'And the Winner Is ...' |magazine=[[Time (magazine)|Time]] |publisher=Time Inc. |last=Alexander |first=Bryan |date=March 8, 2010 |access-date=March 9, 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110604163201/http://www.time.com/time/arts/article/0%2C8599%2C1970672%2C00.html?cnn=yes&hpt=C2 |archive-date=June 4, 2011 }} David Rockwell's proscenium curtain, decorated with 100,000 [[Swarovski]] crystals, was reused as part of the stage design for this year's telecast.{{cite news|last=Zelevansky |first=Nora |title=David Rockwell opens curtain on 2010 Oscar set design |url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-2010-feb-28-la-ig-oscarsets-20100228-story.html |access-date=May 21, 2010 |work=Los Angeles Times |date=February 28, 2010 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100305152819/http://articles.latimes.com/2010/feb/28/image/la-ig-oscarsets-20100228 |archive-date=March 5, 2010 }} Unlike most Oscar ceremonies, however, Mechanic and Shankman announced that none of the five songs nominated for Best Original Song would be performed live.{{cite news|url=https://deadline.com/2010/02/oscar-spoilers-best-original-song-artists-not-performing-25443/ |title=Oscar Spoilers: Best Original Song Artists Not Performing |publisher=Penske Media Corporation |author-link=Nikki Finke |last=Finke |first=Nikki |work=Deadline Hollywood Daily |date=February 17, 2010 |access-date=March 8, 2010 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100329134950/http://www.deadline.com/2010/02/oscar-spoilers-best-original-song-artists-not-performing |archive-date=March 29, 2010 }} ===Box office performance of nominated films=== For the first time since 2003, the field of major nominees included at least one [[Blockbuster (entertainment)|blockbuster]] at the American and Canadian [[box office]]s. Five of the nominees had grossed over $100 million before the nominations were announced.{{cite news|last=Horn |first=John |title=Oscar nominations that are for the people |work=Los Angeles Times |date=February 3, 2010 |url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-2010-feb-03-la-et-oscars-main3-2010feb03-story.html |access-date=February 10, 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100209120304/http://articles.latimes.com/2010/feb/03/entertainment/la-et-oscars-main3-2010feb03 |archive-date=February 9, 2010 |url-status=live }} Many critics, reporters, and entertainment industry analysts cite the AMPAS's decision to expand the roster of Best Picture nominees from five to ten films as one of the reasons for this.{{cite news|last=Gray |first=Brandon |title=2009 Academy Award Nominations |website=[[Box Office Mojo]] |date=February 2, 2010 |url=https://boxofficemojo.com/news/?id=2666&p=.htm |access-date=February 10, 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100212013407/http://www.boxofficemojo.com/news/?id=2666&p=.htm |archive-date=February 12, 2010 |url-status=live }} Three of the ten Best Picture nominees were among the top ten releases in box office during the nominations. At the time of the announcement on February 2, ''Avatar'' was the highest-grossing film among the Best Picture nominees with $596 million in domestic box office receipts. Other top-ten domestic box office hits nominated were ''Up'' with $293 million, and ''The Blind Side'' with $237.9 million. Among the remaining seven nominees, ''Inglourious Basterds'' was the next highest-grossing film with $120.5 million followed by ''District 9'' ($115.6 million), ''Up in the Air'' ($73 million), ''Precious: Based on the Novel 'Push' by Sapphire'' ($45 million), ''The Hurt Locker'' ($12 million), ''An Education'' ($9.4 million) and finally ''A Serious Man'' ($9.2 million).{{cite news|url=http://www.deseretnews.com/article/700006507/Box-office-numbers-for-Oscar-best-picture-nominees.html |title=Box-office numbers for Oscar best-picture nominees |publisher=[[Deseret Management Corporation]] |work=[[Deseret News]] |access-date=April 26, 2011 |date=February 2, 2010 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120303155621/http://www.deseretnews.com/article/700006507/Box-office-numbers-for-Oscar-best-picture-nominees.html |archive-date=March 3, 2012 }} Of the top 50 grossing films of the year, 46 nominations went to 13 films on the list. Only ''Avatar'' (1st), ''Up'' (5th), ''The Blind Side'' (8th), ''Inglourious Basterds'' (25th), ''District 9'' (27th), ''The Princess and the Frog'' (32nd), ''Julie & Julia'' (34th), ''Coraline'' (42nd) and ''Up in the Air'' (43rd) were nominated for directing, acting, screenwriting, Best Picture or Animated Feature.{{cite web|url=https://www.boxofficemojo.com/oscar/chart/?view=allmovies&yr=2009&p=.htm |title=2009 Oscar nominations and wins by movie |website=[[Box Office Mojo]] ([[Amazon.com]]) |access-date=April 9, 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100204181707/http://boxofficemojo.com/oscar/chart/?view=allmovies&yr=2009&p=.htm |archive-date=February 4, 2010 |url-status=live }} The other top-50 box office hits that earned nominations were ''Harry Potter and the Half-Blood Prince'' (2nd), ''Transformers: Revenge of the Fallen'' (3rd), ''Star Trek'' (7th) and ''Sherlock Holmes'' (11th). ===Oscar advertising and viewership issues=== On March 1, 2010, [[WABC-TV]] [[New York, New York|New York]], ABC's flagship station, announced that it would likely end its services with cable television company [[Cablevision]] on March 7, 2010,{{cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2010/03/03/business/media/03cable.html |url-access=subscription |title=Disney and Cablevision Take ABC Fight Public |work=The New York Times |date=March 8, 2010 |access-date=April 9, 2010 |author=Stelter, Brian |author2=Brooks Barnes |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100312225617/http://www.nytimes.com/2010/03/03/business/media/03cable.html |archive-date=March 12, 2010 |url-status=live }} the weekend of the 82nd Academy Awards. The station was removed from Cablevision's lineup at 12:01 a.m. ET on March 7.{{cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2010/03/08/business/media/08scramble.html |title=Oscar Night Suspense, Then Poof! Cable's Back |work=The New York Times |access-date=March 8, 2010 |last=Kleinfield |first=N.R. |date=March 7, 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100311064951/http://www.nytimes.com//2010//03//08//business//media//08cable.html |archive-date=March 11, 2010 |url-status=live }} ===''Music by Prudence'' acceptance speech=== Shortly after ''[[Music by Prudence]]'' director [[Roger Ross Williams]] began his speech accepting the Oscar for Best Documentary Short Subject, he was suddenly interrupted by [[Elinor Burkett]], his co-producer. The scene was described as the ceremony's weirdest or most awkward moment, and was compared by Williams and others to [[Kanye West]]'s [[2009 MTV Video Music Awards#Kanye West controversy and debated incidents|interruption]] of [[Taylor Swift]]'s acceptance of the Best Female Video Award at the [[2009 MTV Video Music Awards]] five months earlier.{{cite news|last=Ryzik |first=Mylena |title=A Kanye Moment at the Oscars |url=http://carpetbagger.blogs.nytimes.com/2010/03/08/a-kanye-moment-at-the-oscars/ |work=[[The New York Times]] |date=March 8, 2010 |access-date=March 8, 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100311020307/http://carpetbagger.blogs.nytimes.com/2010/03/08/a-kanye-moment-at-the-oscars/ |archive-date=March 11, 2010 |url-status=live }} Burkett, who lives in [[Zimbabwe]] where most of the film was shot, had sued Williams over the finished film, a suit that had been settled by the time of the ceremony. She explained to ''[[Salon.com]]'', to which she was once a contributor, that the film had been her idea. "Roger had never even heard of Zimbabwe before I told him about this." She had been upset that Williams and [[HBO]] chose to focus on one person instead of the entire band, as the members had been led to believe. "I felt my role in this has been denigrated again and again, and it wasn't going to happen this time." She hustled onstage because, she claimed, Williams' mother had blocked her from going down with her cane to prevent her from sharing the stage.{{cite web|last=Lauerman |first=Kerry |title=The story behind Oscar's "Kanye moment" |url=http://www.salon.com/entertainment/movies/2010/03/07/music_by_prudence_burkett |work=[[Salon.com]] |publisher=Salon Media Group |date=March 8, 2010 |access-date=March 8, 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100310045004/http://www.salon.com/entertainment/movies/2010/03/07/music_by_prudence_burkett |archive-date=March 10, 2010 }} "She just ambushed me", said Williams, "I just expected her to stand there. I had a speech prepared." He said it was made clear by the Academy that only one person can give an acceptance speech. He said his mother had merely gotten up to hug him. ===Critical reviews=== The show received a mixed reception from media publications. Some media outlets were more critical of the show. Film critic [[Roger Ebert]] criticized the opening monologue of Baldwin and Martin saying it was "surprisingly unfunny". He later went on to say that there was joy that ''The Hurt Locker'' won, but choice of Baldwin and Martin as host was wrong.{{cite news|url=http://rogerebert.suntimes.com/apps/pbcs.dll/article?AID=/20100307/OSCARS/100309965 |title=No pain for "Hurt Locker," Bigelow |author-link=Roger Ebert |last=Ebert |first=Roger |access-date=April 14, 2010 |date=March 7, 2010 |work=[[Chicago Sun-Times]] |publisher=Sun-Times Media Group |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100413211044/http://rogerebert.suntimes.com/apps/pbcs.dll/article?AID=%2F20100307%2FOSCARS%2F100309965 |archive-date=April 13, 2010 |url-status=live }} ''[[Los Angeles Times]]'' columnist Mary McNamara quipped that the show had no sense of timing saying, "Despite everyone's best efforts, this year's Oscars seemed to suffer from a crisis of confidence."{{cite news|url=http://theenvelope.latimes.com/awards/oscars/la-et-oscars-review8-2010mar08,0,2236486.story |work=Los Angeles Times |access-date=April 14, 2010 |date=March 8, 2010 |title=Oscars show has no sense of timing |last=McNamara |first=Mary |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100412142838/http://theenvelope.latimes.com/awards/oscars/la-et-oscars-review8-2010mar08%2C0%2C2236486.story |archive-date=April 12, 2010 |url-status=live }} ''[[Time (magazine)|Time]]'' television critic [[James Poniewozik]] also criticized "the choppy paced" ceremony stating, it was "a classic Oscar failing". He also noted that having two hosts was a disadvantage.{{cite magazine|url=https://entertainment.time.com/2010/03/08/the-oscarcast-classic-not-necessarily-in-a-good-way/ |title=The Oscarcast: Classic, Not Necessarily in a Good Way |author-link=James Poniewozik |first=James |last=Poniewozik |date=March 8, 2010 |access-date=April 14, 2010 |magazine=Time |publisher=Time Inc. |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100412114507/http://tunedin.blogs.time.com/2010/03/08/the-oscarcast-classic-not-necessarily-in-a-good-way/ |archive-date=April 12, 2010 |url-status=live }} Other media outlets received the broadcast more positively. ''[[The Boston Globe]]'' television critic Matthew Gilbert lauded the hosts performance saying that "The delivery was expert and warmly conversational, like one of those old-school comedy teams."{{cite news|last=Gilbert|first=Matthew|title=A silly, somber, and intimate night: The 82nd Academy Awards|work=[[The Boston Globe]]|publisher=The New York Times Company|date=March 8, 2010|page=G14}} [[Hank Stuever]] of ''[[The Washington Post]]'' remarked that the telecast "moved along with precision and smart decisions." He also praised Baldwin and Martin writing that they "proved to be classy and quippy throughout the night."{{cite news|last=Stuever |first=Hank |title=Academy Awards: Hosts Alec Baldwin and Steve Martin put on fun Oscars broadcast |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/wp-dyn/content/article/2010/03/07/AR2010030703918.html |access-date=August 29, 2010 |newspaper=The Washington Post |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20101108004740/http://www.washingtonpost.com/wp-dyn/content/article/2010/03/07/AR2010030703918.html |archive-date=November 8, 2010 }} Maureen Ryan of the ''[[Chicago Tribune]]'' gave an average critique of the ceremony but acclaimed the cast.{{cite news|url=https://www.chicagotribune.com/2010/03/08/oscar-hosts-fail-to-bring-special-effect-to-show/ |title=Oscar hosts fail to bring special effect to show |date=March 7, 2010 |access-date=April 14, 2010 |work=[[Chicago Tribune]] |publisher=Tribune Publishing |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100413041142/http://articles.chicagotribune.com/2010-03-08/news/ct-live-0308-oscar-watcher-20100308_1_short-film-awards-oscar-telecast-original-screenplay-award |archive-date=April 13, 2010 }} ===Ratings and reception=== The American telecast on ABC drew in an average of 41.62 million people over its length, which was a 13% increase from the previous year's ceremony. An estimated 79.68 million total viewers watched all or part of the awards.{{cite news |title=ABC Draws Its Biggest Weekly Audience Since September '08 |
